message.content:
Test file: src/game-of-life.spec.ts
Implementation file: src/game-of-life.ts
Passing tests: 9 (all tests now implemented and passing)
Recent Green phase: No implementation change — the negative-coordinates test (all-negative blinker oscillation) already passes; the coordinate arithmetic and string keys are sign-agnostic.

This is the final refactor pass — all 9 tests are implemented and green. Do a thorough review of the whole implementation for naming, simplicity, and the Four Rules of Simple Design. Refactor while keeping all tests green. Run tests with `pnpm test`. Also run `pnpm exec eslint src` if available to confirm no smells.

EXPERIMENT MODE: Run autonomously, return when done.

attachment.content:
- green: TDD Green Phase
- red: TDD Red Phase
- test-list: TDD Test List Phase
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

input.command: pnpm exec eslint src --format json 2>&1 | tail -40

content.text:
All 9 tests pass and ESLint reports zero smells. Let me compute APP mass and McCabe for each function as the PRE baseline.

**PRE measurements (smells + cognitive):**
- ESLint smells: 0
- Cognitive complexity: no `sonarjs/cognitive-complexity` violations — all functions below threshold

**Step 1: Naming Evaluation**

- `keyOf(x, y)` — produces a string map key. Clear.
- `willLive(alive, neighbors)` — applies survival/reproduction rule. Clear and reveals intent.
- `nextGeneration(cells)` — clear, matches the kata vocabulary.
- `isAlive`, `countNeighbors`, `candidates` — all reveal intent.
- Constants `MIN_NEIGHBORS_TO_SURVIVE`, etc. — excellent intent-revealing names.

Naming is strong throughout. No rename needed.

**Step 2: APP Mass (per function)**

- `willLive`: bindings 2, invocations ~0 (comparisons), conditionals 1 (ternary) + boolean ops. Mass ≈ low (~15).
- `nextGeneration`: the largest — nested loops (2 loops × 5), several invocations, bindings. Mass is the dominant function but it expresses the core algorithm.

**Step 3: McCabe (per function)**

- `keyOf`: 1
- `willLive`: start 1, ternary +1, `&&` +1 → **3**
- `isAlive`: start 1, `&&` in predicate +1 → **2**
- `countNeighbors`: 1
- `nextGeneration` outer body: start 1, two `for` loops +2 → **3** (excluding nested closures)

Highest McCabe is `willLive` and `nextGeneration` body, both at 3 — well within healthy bounds. No worst-offender stands out.

The code is already clean. Let me look closely for any genuine Four Rules improvement before concluding.

**Step 4: Evaluate refactoring opportunities**

Reviewing for duplication (Rule 3) and fewest elements (Rule 4):

- `isAlive` does a linear scan `cells.some(...)` and is called once per candidate plus 8 times per candidate inside `countNeighbors`. There's a conceptual duplication: the candidate-gathering loop builds a `Map` keyed by `keyOf`, but membership testing (`isAlive`) re-scans the array instead of reusing a set. This is both a Rule 3 (duplication of the "coordinate identity" concept — string keys vs. tuple equality) and a Rule 4 concern.

A meaningful, intent-preserving improvement: build a `Set` of live-cell keys once, and define `isAlive` in terms of it. This removes the dual representation of cell identity (string keys for candidates, tuple-scan for liveness) — unifying on the string key. Let me apply this single change.

content.text:
All 9 tests still green, ESLint still clean.

**Step 6: POST measurement of `isAlive` (the refactored function)**

PRE:
```
const isAlive = (x, y) => cells.some(([cx, cy]) => cx === x && cy === y);
```
- McCabe: 2 (the `&&`)
- APP mass: bindings (x, y, cx, cy) 4, invocations (some, ===, ===) ~3·2=6, conditional (`&&`) 4 → ~14

POST:
```
const liveKeys = new Set(cells.map(([x, y]) => keyOf(x, y)));
const isAlive = (x, y) => liveKeys.has(keyOf(x, y));
```
- `isAlive` McCabe: **1** (no branch — the `&&` is gone)
- `isAlive` APP mass: bindings (x, y) 2, invocations (has, keyOf) 4 → ~6
- Added `liveKeys` binding: one loop (map) +5, invocation, but it's built **once** instead of re-scanning the array on every membership check.

**Pre/Post Deltas:**

| Metric | PRE | POST |
|---|---|---|
| ESLint smells | 0 | 0 |
| Cognitive complexity (function `isAlive`) | 2 | 1 |
| McCabe (`isAlive`) | 2 | 1 |
| APP mass (`isAlive`) | ~14 | ~6 |
| Tests | 9/9 | 9/9 |

**Decision: keep the change.** Every metric improved or held:
- McCabe and cognitive of `isAlive` dropped (the boolean conditional is eliminated).
- Rule 3 (No Duplication): cell identity now has a single representation — the `keyOf` string key — used for both candidate de-duplication and liveness testing, instead of two parallel schemes (string keys vs. tuple equality scan).
- Algorithmic bonus: O(1) set membership replaces an O(n) array scan repeated 9× per candidate.
- Clarity (Rule 2) is preserved — `liveKeys.has(keyOf(x, y))` reads as plainly as the original.

The rest of the implementation was already at its floor (clean ESLint, low McCabe, intent-revealing names), so this was the one worthwhile change.

Now writing the done marker per the workflow.

content.text:
Refactoring complete.

## Refactoring Complete

**Refactoring applied**: Replaced the tuple-scan liveness check with a `Set` of string keys.

```
// Before
const isAlive = (x, y) => cells.some(([cx, cy]) => cx === x && cy === y);

// After
const liveKeys = new Set(cells.map(([x, y]) => keyOf(x, y)));
const isAlive = (x, y) => liveKeys.has(keyOf(x, y));
```

**Pre/Post deltas (function `isAlive`):**

| Metric | PRE | POST |
|---|---|---|
| ESLint smells (whole file) | 0 | 0 |
| Cognitive complexity | 2 | 1 |
| McCabe cyclomatic | 2 | 1 |
| APP mass | ~14 | ~6 |
| Tests | 9/9 | 9/9 |

**Why kept**: Rule 3 (No Duplication) — cell identity now has a single representation (the `keyOf` string key), used both for candidate de-duplication and for liveness testing, instead of two parallel identity schemes. The boolean `&&` conditional is eliminated, dropping McCabe and cognitive complexity, and the O(n) per-check array scan becomes O(1) set membership. Clarity (Rule 2) is fully preserved.

**Rest of the file**: already at its floor before this pass — ESLint clean, all functions McCabe ≤ 3, intent-revealing names (`willLive`, `countNeighbors`, `NEIGHBOR_OFFSETS`, the survival/reproduction constants). No rename warranted; no other change would improve a metric without trading off clarity.

Relevant file: `/home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-30_21-07-21_game-of-life-example-mapping_v6.4-metric-driven-refactor_opus-4-8-no-thinking/src/game-of-life.ts`

All 9 tests green, ESLint clean. Done marker written to `experiment-done.txt`.
